item.title: Impressions of Norda: Obsessed with Excellence and Hospitality
item.itunes:title: Impressions of Norda: Obsessed with Excellence and Hospitality
item.description:
Norda punches like a $50M company with the values of a local small business.

Norda Run is redefining the running super shoe with its upcoming 005 trail running 'Race Days' shoe. But they've already built a wildly committed fan base with their 001, 002, + 003.

Norda is as cool of a company as it is high quality. Yes, their shoes are functionally in the top of the class, but they carry a cool factor that few running shoe companies can match.

I spent a day with them at Paris Fashion Week 2025 and not only observed a company growing exponentially but one that also carries a hospitality and warmth like few brands I've seen.

These are my impressions of Norda.

  1. 00:16 Partners
  2. 1:19 I'm a Champion of Brands, not a critic
  3. 3:00 Surface level contradictions
  4. 6:45 Product Market Fit, is your hair on fire
  5. 9:39 Innovation in a comples supply chain
  6. 12:38 Balance of Luxury, Premium, Value
  7. 19:50 Norda is a natural expression of Nick + Willa

---- Partners ----

Naked Sports Innovations - the worlds best hydration belt

TMRW Coffee - Code Borderlands for 20% off

Salt Lake Footshills Trail Races. Salt Lake City, UT - May 31, 2025

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

item.title: Fuel Your Run: Fast Nutrition Tips for Runners with Jesse Rich
item.itunes:title: Fuel Your Run: Fast Nutrition Tips for Runners with Jesse Rich
item.description:
Jesse Rich is a coach, nutritionist, and ultrarunner based in Salt Lake City, Utah. He brings a great perspective to nutrition and is one of the most honest brokers I've seen in the nutrition space.

  1. Nutrition is as valuable as training for race success.
  2. Many runners are under fueling, impacting their performance.
  3. High carb intake can significantly improve race outcomes.
  4. Diet quality affects recovery and overall running performance.
  5. Alcohol can hinder recovery and sleep quality.
  6. Real food alternatives can be cost-effective for runners.
  7. Carb loading should enhance performance, not hinder it.
  8. 02:14 The Importance of Nutrition in Ultra Running
  9. 04:57 Navigating the Nutrition Landscape
  10. 10:19 Understanding Running Nutrition Trends
  11. 15:22 Diet and Running Performance
  12. 19:12 Alcohol and Running Recovery
  13. 24:12 Race Day Preparation
  14. 29:22 Off-Season Diet
  15. 34:32 Nutrition at High Altitudes

item.title: New Trail Running League + Angermund Reaction | Bad Runners Take
item.itunes:title: New Trail Running League + Angermund Reaction | Bad Runners Take
item.description:
In this episode of the Borderlands Trail and Ultra Running podcast, host Josh Rosenthal (founder of Borderlands)and guest Wolfrunner delve into the complexities of clean sport, race pricing, and the evolving landscape of competitive running.

As race prices increase runners will be looking for alternatives - enter a new trail running league.

Weigh in on a futue episode at RUNMORE649 (786 667 3649). Let us know your take on our Bad Runners Take.

item.title: Billy Yang | An Interview with the Best Running Filmmaker
item.itunes:title: Billy Yang | An Interview with the Best Running Filmmaker
item.description:
‪@BillyYang‬ makes some of the most iconic and memorable trail running films of all time. The Why • Life in a Day • UTMB (aka Nike Boys) • Zach • Western Time.

(this episode is even better on YouTube - watch here)

The Why helped a generation of ultra trail runners show their friends and family what they're doing out on the ultra trails.

Billy is advocate for the preservation of what keeps this sport beautiful and appealing and it clearly shows in this interview.

item.title: How to Keep Running When You Want to Quit, Late Race Survival.
item.itunes:title: How to Keep Running When You Want to Quit, Late Race Survival.
item.description:
In 2023, Michael Whiteside ran two crazy races back to back called the Bear 100 and Wasatch 100. Two iconic and storied Utah races that embody the romance of the American Ultra scene like few others.

They’re big mountain races.

I originally recorded this interview a year ago as I was trying to finish a hundred miler. At the time I had DNFed 8 of 9 100 milers and was obsessed with talking to people like Michael Whiteside to figure out how he finishes 100 milers and thought his huge back to back effort embodied a mindset that I needed.

Trail Grit, Episode 4 - Zion 100 Miler

----

item.title: [Wolfie's World] Why Trail Runner of the Year Misses the Mark.
item.itunes:title: [Wolfie's World] Why Trail Runner of the Year Misses the Mark.
item.description:
Trail Runner of the Year (TROY) is well intentioned but it asks too much of fans and shows that trail running content in general has room to grow.

The scope is global and fans simply aren't aware enough of the global talent pool and performances of trail races around the globe.

  1. Why are the industry content creators missing what fans want?
  2. Where is the entertainment?

Brian doesn't just tear it down, he rebuilds.

  1. What could entertainment look like in trail running?
  2. How do we bring back the "E"?

Wolfie's World is a weekly monologue by Brian Peterson (@wolfrunner) on the Borderlands Platfrom. Borderlands doesn't exercize any editorial contral. It provides that platform and allows Brian to say whatever he wants.

----

Call RUNMORE649 (786-667-3649). Leave a message for the podcast—hot takes, agreement, anger, or joy.

----

Salt Lake Footshills Trail Races. Salt Lake City, UT - May 31, 2025

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

item.title: Training for Success in Your A Race: Strategies for Ultra Races
item.itunes:title: Training for Success in Your A Race: Strategies for Ultra Races
item.description:
Tommy Lewis of That's Runnable + Heidi Baker of Let's Run that Again join Josh to talk 2025 race goals.

Heidi and Tommy share an A Race in common - Devil's Gulch 100 Miler. I visited that race last year and made a video about it. I'm pumped for them.

Josh is going to run the Paris Marathon and he's on the waitlist for Paris Ecotrail 80km.

  1. The best outcomes of consuming content are to inspire action.
  2. The culture of ultra running in America has a unique romance.
  3. Smaller races can provide a more intimate and humble experience.
  4. Experiencing trails is valuable, even with DNFs.
  5. The journey to a 100-mile race is as important as the race itself. If I was seeking external validation, I would have given up.
  6. Running across Texas is a personal ambition.

----

Call RUNMORE649 (786-667-3649). Leave a message for the podcast—hot takes, agreement, anger, or joy.

----

item.title: Rethinking the Running Community (and why it matters): the Future of Borderlands
item.itunes:title: Rethinking the Running Community (and why it matters): the Future of Borderlands
item.description:
This is what Borderlands Trail Running will look like in the next year and where it will be in 10 years.

  1. Podcast: I will 10x the listeners in 2025 and move from it being all about me to being all about 'we'. I'll build a team of media personalities and distribute them through Borderlands.
  2. Tech: Wylder App - I will release v1.0 + 2.0 in 2025
  3. Events: Salt Lake Foothills Trail Races
  4. Robie House: I will build the ultimate pre-race media beginning at WSER 2025
  5. Apparel: I'm looking for a non-tech apparel company to partner with on an equity level in 2025.
  6. Music: I will release one new song per month

----

podcast:alternateEnclosure.title: Rethinking the Running Community (and why it matters)

item.title: Trail Running Brotherhood: Wolfie's World
item.itunes:title: Trail Running Brotherhood: Wolfie's World
item.description:
In this episode, the Brian reflects on the past year, sharing personal insights and nostalgic memories related to BMX bikes and their representation of freedom during youth.

The conversation transitions into the theme of purposeful brotherhood, exploring how these connections formed in childhood can carry into adulthood, particularly through activities like trail and ultra running.

The importance of community and connection among men is emphasized, highlighting the value of deep relationships and the role of trail running in fostering these bonds. The episode concludes with a call to protect the ethos of the sport and the brotherhood it cultivates.

  1. Trail running fosters community and connection among men.
  2. Nostalgia plays a significant role in our identities.
  3. The importance of self-expression in sports and hobbies.
  4. Men's friendships can thrive even in isolation.
  5. Understanding women's perspectives in trail running is valuable.
  6. Protecting the ethos of trail running is crucial for its future.

----

Call RUNMORE649 (786-667-3649). Leave a message for the podcast—hot takes, agreement, anger, or joy.

----

Salt Lake Footshills Trail Races. Salt Lake City, UT - May 31, 2025

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

item.title: Best Trail Running Shoes and a peek at 2025
item.itunes:title: Best Trail Running Shoes and a peek at 2025
item.description:
Taylor and I look at Believe in the Run's Best Trail Running Shoes or 2024 rated by the Trail Running team.

  1. Trail Running Growth: The industry is experiencing significant growth, with increasing investment signaling a vibrant future.
  2. Gear Innovation: Shoe technology, such as foam advancements and door-to-trail designs, is evolving to meet diverse needs and improve the running experience.
  3. Celebrating Culture: Preserving the culture and community of trail running, including its non-elite aspects, is crucial for continued growth.
  4. Consumer Choices: Informed decisions in gear selection, especially shoes, are vital for enhancing performance and reducing runner anxiety.
  5. Brand Leadership and Impact: Emerging brands and innovations, like La Sportiva and Craft, are making waves, while established names like Hoka face scrutiny over their offerings.

----

item.title: Think Speed to Run Faster: Wolfie's World
item.itunes:title: Think Speed to Run Faster: Wolfie's World
item.description:
In this episode of Wolfies World, host Brian delves into the mental aspects of ultra running, focusing on the concepts of speed and fast.

Brian emphasizes the importance of speed workouts in training and encourages listeners to redefine their relationship with speed, moving away from comparisons and self-deprecation.

  1. Running is about personal improvement, not competition with others.
  2. Speed workouts are essential for becoming a better ultra runner.
  3. It's important to decouple speed from the concept of being fast.
  4. Speed workouts sharpen mental strength and running mechanics.
  5. Enjoying running is key to long-term participation in the sport.

----

In this episode of Wolfies World, host Brian delves into the mental aspects of ultra running, focusing on the concepts of speed and fast.

Brian emphasizes the importance of speed workouts in training and encourages listeners to redefine their relationship with speed, moving away from comparisons and self-deprecation.

  1. Running is about personal improvement, not competition with others.
  2. Speed workouts are essential for becoming a better ultra runner.
  3. It's important to decouple speed from the concept of being fast.
  4. Speed workouts sharpen mental strength and running mechanics.
  5. Enjoying running is key to long-term participation in the sport.

----

item.itunes:title: The Secret to Ultra Running: Unshakeable Confidence [Wolfie's World]
item.description:
Welcome to another episode of Wolfie's World of Running with Brian Peterson.

in today's monologue emphasizes that mental strength and self-belief are foundational to achieving success on race day.

He discusses the often-overlooked importance of cultivating confidence, suggesting that many athletes show up malnourished in this regard.

  1. Confidence fuels mental strength, essential for ultra running success.
  2. Mental preparation is as crucial as physical training for races.
  3. Community support boosts confidence before ultra running events.
  4. Challenging workouts build mental resilience for endurance races.
  5. Discipline enhances confidence in both running and life.

item.description:
In this episode, Taylor Bodin of Believe in the Run joins me to help him find the right shoe.

I honestly don't enjoy buying gear. I want to have great gear, but I loathe the process of finding it which usually leads to poor gear choices.

  1. Tips on finding the right shoe
  2. Tips on finding cheaper shoes?

  4. Thoughts on how a shoe should fit
  5. Expertise on how several shoe brands actually fit.

Brands mentioned:

Topo Athletic, Nike, Speedland, Norda, North Face, and more.

He's a firehouse of insight and information and we're all better that he's focusing that energy on trail running through his recent aquisition of Rogue Expedition.

  2. Less racing, more running together.
  3. Self-expression will drive growth over performance.

item.title: Why We Run: The Misunderstood Motivations of Trail Runners
item.itunes:title: Why We Run: The Misunderstood Motivations of Trail Runners
item.description:
Wolfie and I delve into the world of trail running to uncover the motivations that drive these athletes to push themselves to the limit.

It's not what you think. And Wolfie challenged me to rethink some things as usual.

We look at three distinct buckets of motivation - self-expression, performance, + health.

What are the secrets behind their passion? Let's find out.

Takeaways

• More runners than ever run as a means of self-expression

• Wolfie's World of Running, a weekly monologue by Wolfrunner

• More on why Matt Choi should be reinstated to NYC Marathon

• Road Ultra Shoe category is more marketing than function

----

item.title: The Post-Covid Trail Shoe Awakening: NNormal, Speedland, + Norda
item.itunes:title: The Post-Covid Trail Shoe Awakening: NNormal, Speedland, + Norda
item.description:
I went into this conversation ready to hate on NNormal and Taylor Bodin (lead trail reviewer of Believe in the Run) changed my mind.

  1. Trail Shoe Innovation: Brands like NNormal, Speedland, and Norda focus on durability and advanced materials like Dyneema and Vibram.
  2. Brand Differentiation: Niche brands are gaining ground with unique technologies and strong community ties.
  3. Performance Focus: Speedland’s Boa system and Michelin outsoles enhance fit, traction, and performance.
  4. Sustainability & Resources: NNormal’s partnership with Camper emphasizes eco-friendly production and resources.
  5. Market Renaissance: The trail shoe market is booming, offering a mix of performance and innovative design.

----

Believe in the Run reviews:

NNormal: Tomir 2.0, Kjerag

Speedland: GS:TAM, GS:PGH, SL:HSV

Norda: Norda 001, Norda 002, Norda 003

----

Call RUNMORE649 (786-667-3649). Leave a message for the podcast—hot takes, agreement, anger, or joy.

----

Salt Lake Footshills Trail Races. Salt Lake City, UT - May 31, 2025

item.title: The Unexpected Joy of Urban Running
item.itunes:title: The Unexpected Joy of Urban Running
item.description:
How do trail runners survive in cities? Josh Rosenthal and Tommy Lewis dive into the challenges and rewards of maintaining a running lifestyle in urban environments.

They discuss the unique rhythms of road running compared to trail running, exploring how each offers different forms of joy and fulfillment. Tommy shares his journey from trail enthusiast to urban runner, emphasizing the importance of redefining what it means to be a runner beyond traditional races like marathons.

Together, they highlight the beauty of finding community, appreciating the outdoors, and embracing the daily practice of running, regardless of the terrain or distance.

Tommy Lewis took the dive and left his dayjob to lean all the way in on ThatsRunnable.com. He writes a brilliant weekly newlsetter and speaks to heart of runners like few others can.

----

Call the Borderlands Hotline - RUNMORE649 That's (786)667-3649. Leave a voice message I can play in a future podcast. We're looking for hot takes, disagreement, agreement, anger, happiness, indifference, etc.

----

Devil's Gulch 100 miler, 50 miler, 13.1 miles. Wenatchee, WA - July 13, 2025

Salt Lake Footshills Trail Races. Salt Lake City, UT - May 31, 2025

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run, 20% off.

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

---

Borderlands

Instagram

YouTube

Threads

LinkedIn

Takeaways:

  1. Finding joy in running is about defining it on your own terms, not others'.
  2. The community aspect of running can enhance the experience, regardless of the environment.
  3. Many runners mistakenly believe they must run marathons to be considered legitimate runners.
  4. Incorporating variety in training, such as mixing road and trail runs, is crucial.
  5. Embracing the daily practice of running fosters a lifelong love for the sport.

item.title: The Problem with Ambassador Culture in the Running Community: BAD RUNNERS TAKE
item.itunes:title: The Problem with Ambassador Culture in the Running Community: BAD RUNNERS TAKE
item.description:
Wolfrunner (Brian Peterson) and Josh Rosenthal (host) discuss the problems with ambassador culture in this episode of Bad Runners Take.

  1. Ambassador culture in running blends brand connections with potential entitlement.
  2. Brands use ambassadorships as a marketing tactic but must manage expectations.
  3. Norda fosters organic, personal connections, avoiding formal ambassador structures.
  4. Running has become a consumer-driven sport, with influencers and ambassadorships shaping its culture.
  5. Content creation and community events are key to the future of trail running.

----

item.title: BAD RUNNERS TAKE: Breaking Down DNF Culture: Why We Need to Celebrate Our Miles.
item.itunes:title: BAD RUNNERS TAKE: Breaking Down DNF Culture: Why We Need to Celebrate Our Miles.
item.description:
This podcast episode dives deep into the culture surrounding DNFs (Did Not Finish) in ultra running, with hosts Brian and Josh discussing the social constructs and emotional weight attached to these outcomes.

Brian passionately argues that runners should be able to celebrate their efforts, regardless of whether they complete a race, pointing out the absurdity of labeling a significant distance as a failure.

The conversation expands to explore the pressures and expectations placed on amateur runners compared to elite athletes, highlighting the different motivations and mentalities in play.

Additionally, the hosts reflect on the implications of race metrics and how the ultra running community could evolve to better recognize the achievements of those who may not finish but still push their limits. With a mix of humor and serious reflection, the episode encourages listeners to reconsider how they perceive and react to DNFs in the sport.

----

Devil's Gulch 100 miler, 50 miler, 13.1 miles. Wenatchee, WA - July 13, 2025

Salt Lake Footshills Trail Races. Salt Lake City, UT - May 31, 2025

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run, 20% off.

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

---

Borderlands

Instagram

YouTube

Threads

LinkedIn

Takeaways:

  1. The DNF culture in ultra running creates unnecessary stigma around not finishing races.
  2. Runners invest significant time and effort into training, making DNFs feel like failures.
  3. There should be alternative metrics for race completion rather than just DNF statuses.
  4. Celebrating accomplishments in running, even if they don't result in a finish, is important.
  5. The podcast discusses how elite runners view DNFs differently than amateur runners.
  6. There is a need for a cultural shift in how we perceive and discuss DNFs.

item.title: Music Inspired by Trail Running Suffering
item.itunes:title: Music Inspired by Trail Running Suffering
item.description:
In Episode 100 I introduced 4 songs along with their stories. In this episode I offer brief introduction and give you the music back to back without interuption.

I'm calling this music project CAVALE. And this group of songs is called 'Into the Virgin Desert'.

item.title: Ultra Runners on a Sanctioned Russian Plane to Antarctica: Exposing the Controversy
item.itunes:title: Ultra Runners on a Sanctioned Russian Plane to Antarctica: Exposing the Controversy
item.description:
In this episode we talk about articles by Marley for Canadian Running Magazine. In my opinion there is no better magazine on running news.

  1. U.S. running tourism company using disguised Russian plane for Antarctic race
  2. Ruth Chepngetich shatters women’s world record at Chicago Marathon
  3. U.S. ultrarunner Camille Herron involved in Wikipedia controversy
  4. Running influencer Matt Choi disqualified from New York City Marathon

The latest episode of the Borderlands Trail podcast dives deep into the complexities of recent stories in the running world, including a significant discussion on the implications of using pacers in marathon races, highlighted by Ruth Chepngetich's record-setting performance in Chicago.

Host Josh Rosenthal and journalist Marley Dickinson explore how the use of pacers can alter the nature of competition, raising questions about authenticity and strategy in elite races. The conversation also touches on the ongoing saga surrounding Camille Herron and the fallout from her recent controversies, as well as a scandal involving a Russian airline used for a major marathon event.

The duo examines the broader implications for athletes and the sport, including the potential for increased scrutiny and the ethics of competitive running. As they unpack these narratives, listeners are invited to consider what defines true competition in the world of athletics.

----

item.title: HOKA Revolution: How a Brand Changed Trail Running Forever
item.itunes:title: HOKA Revolution: How a Brand Changed Trail Running Forever
item.description:
Hoka is directly repsonsible for much of the exponential growth of trail running.

The Best Hoka Running Shoes Right Now.

Josh Rosenthal and Taylor Bodin (Lead Trail Reviewer, Believe in the Run) dive deep into the world of HOKA, a brand that has revolutionized trail and ultra running with its innovative footwear.

They discuss how HOKA emerged during a time dominated by minimalist trends, introducing a maximalist approach that has reshaped expectations in the running community.

Taylor shares insights about the evolution of HOKA's shoe technology, including the introduction of high stack cushioning and rocker designs that enhance performance. The conversation highlights the brand’s impressive growth, underscored by their recent success in exceeding sales projections, and its impact on the broader trail running landscape.

Throughout their discussion, Josh and Taylor emphasize HOKA's commitment to the running community, showcasing how the brand continues to engage with athletes and enthusiasts alike.

----

[Sponsor] Sealevel Insights: Imagine mastering your small business finances.

Devil's Gulch 100 miler, 50 miler, 13.1 miles. Wenatchee, WA - July 13, 2025

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

Takeaways:

  • HOKA has revolutionized the ultra trail running market with its maximalist shoe design.
  • The brand's unique approach contrasts sharply with the minimalist trend popularized by Born to Run.
  • HOKA's deep connection to the community is evident through their support of events like Devil's Gulch 100 Miler.
  • Taylor Bodine's insights provide a comprehensive view of HOKA's shoe technology evolution since 2009.
  • The Speedgoat shoe, named after Carl Meltzer, is a favorite among ultra runners for its performance.
  • HOKA's success is attributed to its innovative marketing strategies and commitment to quality gear.

item.title: 200-Milers: the New Frontier or a Distraction?
item.itunes:title: 200-Milers: the New Frontier or a Distraction?
item.description:
Today on Bad Runners Take:

  1. Are 200-mile races becoming a distraction or the main attraction in the ultra running community?
  2. How do you feel about profitable races existing on volunteer labor?
  3. Josh got into the Paris Marathon. Why is it so hard for trail runners to run on the road for a season?

Josh Rosenthal and Brian Peterson dive into these questions as they explore the growing prominence of these long-distance events.

They discuss the implications for elite athletes like Courtney Dauwalter, who is preparing for Cocodona 250, and how such races might shift focus away from traditional 100-mile competitions.

The conversation also touches on the culture of volunteerism in the sport, particularly in relation to profitable races, and the balance between community support and financial gain.

As the two navigate these topics, they reflect on their own running experiences and the evolving landscape of trail and ultra running.

----

Devil's Gulch 100 miler, 50 miler, 13.1 miles. Wenatchee, WA - July 13, 2025

Salt Lake Footshills Trail Races. Salt Lake City, UT - May 31, 2025

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run, 20% off.

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

---

  • The conversation discusses whether 200-mile races distract from the main attraction of ultra running.
  • Courtney's participation in Cocodona illustrates the growing popularity of 200-mile races.
  • Volunteers play a crucial role in the success of ultra running events, yet compensation raises questions.
  • Josh reflects on the conflict between road marathons and his identity as a trail runner.
  • The evolution of running shoes from durability to performance models has shifted expectations.
  • There's a debate on whether elite runners should focus solely on traditional 100-milers.

item.title: The Best Races Under 10 miles: Cirque Series founder Julian Carr
item.itunes:title: The Best Races Under 10 miles: Cirque Series founder Julian Carr
item.description:
Cirque Series is a brilliant mountain race with distances between 6 and 9 miles. But don't be fooled ultrarunners. Cirque races are hard as hell, straight up the gut of big mountains and right back down.

The starting line is a party.

The finish line is a party.

In this episode I welcome the founder and race director, Julian Carr.

----

In this conversation, Josh and Julian discuss the evolution of ultra trail running, focusing on the Cirque Series and its unique approach to inclusivity and race production.

They explore the importance of creating a fun and engaging atmosphere at races, overcoming skepticism from the traditional trail running community, and the significance of high production value in enhancing the overall experience for runners.

In this conversation, Julian discusses the evolution of trail running events, emphasizing the importance of a rewarding experience for participants. He reflects on the challenges of creating a unique atmosphere that transcends traditional trail running, while also addressing the scalability of his event series and the significance of community engagement and sponsorship.

The discussion highlights the cultural movement of trail running in the U.S. and the potential for storytelling to enhance the experience for both elite and amateur runners.

----

Salt Lake Foothills Trail Races - May 31

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

item.title: Two running empires, Adidas vs Puma. The famliy feud + the current trail shoe portfolio.
item.itunes:title: Two running empires, Adidas vs Puma. The famliy feud + the current trail shoe portfolio.
item.description:
Thomas Neuberger joined me from Believe in the Run to talk about the familiy feud that built two great running empires. Puma + Adidas.

Rudi and Adi were the perfect team until they weren't. They spent most of their adult years at odds and eventually were buried at opposite ends of the same cemetary.

We dig into the family feud and talk about the current road and trail portfolio.

More from Believe in the Run:

Adidas Adizero Evo SL Review

A comprehensive overview of Adidas' shoe portfolio.

item.title: Matt Johnson's Run Across Texas + Sally McRae's Mammoth Project
item.itunes:title: Matt Johnson's Run Across Texas + Sally McRae's Mammoth Project
item.description:
In this conversation, Josh and Drew Darby discuss Matt Johnson's journey across Texas in 17 days and Sally McRae's 200 mile Mammoth Project.

Drew Darby was there for both.

They explore the challenges faced during the adventures, the dynamics of being a cameraman, and the unique character of Matt as a hybrid athlete.

The discussion also touches on the mindset required for endurance sports, Project Mammoth featuring Sally McCrae, and what lies ahead for Matt in his athletic career. In this conversation, Drew and Josh delve into the intricacies of documenting endurance sports, exploring the balance between storytelling and authenticity.

They discuss the evolution of endurance events, the creative process behind filmmaking, and the impact of media on athletes' performances. Drew shares insights on capturing genuine moments while navigating the challenges of filming in high-pressure environments.

The conversation highlights the importance of long-term relationships in filmmaking and the unique experiences that arise during endurance challenges.

item.title: Do I Have Trail Running Credibility?
item.itunes:title: Do I Have Trail Running Credibility?
item.description:
Does Josh have the necessary credibility to build a trail running media company? He's not a former pro runner or even a good runner. In fact he chases cutoffs most of the time. He only has one elite trail running friend. Does he belong at the table?

In this episode of the Borderlands podcast, host Wolfrunner interviews Josh Rosenthal, exploring his journey from Lubbock, Texas to Salt Lake City. They discuss Josh's upbringing, the cultural dynamics of Lubbock, his musical influences, and the impact of geography on happiness and identity.

The conversation delves into the importance of boredom in fostering creativity and the contrasting experiences of growing up in different environments. In this conversation, Josh shares his journey of moving to Salt Lake City, building connections through running, and navigating the challenges of entrepreneurship.

He reflects on the unique sports culture in Salt Lake, the emotional power of running, and the importance of supporting others in their dreams. Josh discusses his musical aspirations, the entrepreneurial spirit he developed during his punk rock days, and how he discovered a passion for finance.

He emphasizes the significance of marketing in sharing creations and the methodical approach he took towards achieving his ultra running goals. In this conversation, Josh shares his experiences with running, entrepreneurship, and the challenges of balancing passion with business.

He discusses the importance of relationships in building a successful venture and reflects on the dynamics of the trail running community.

The conversation also touches on the significance of credibility, authority, and relatability in the running space, as well as the role of luck in achieving success.

----

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run, 20% off.

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

---

item.title: The Brooks Running Enigma: Why does Brooks fly under the radar?
item.itunes:title: The Brooks Running Enigma: Why does Brooks fly under the radar?
item.description:
Brooks Running remains a prominent name in the footwear industry, yet it often feels like an enigma within the trail running community.

Josh Rosenthal and Taylor Bodin (Lead Trail Reviewer at BiTR) dive deep into this paradox, exploring why, despite their widespread presence and extensive history, Brooks doesn't quite resonate as a go-to brand for trail runners.

Believe in the Run - Catamount 3

BiTR - Caldera 7

BiTR - Catamount Agil

BiTR - Brooks Divide 5

BiTR - Cascadia 18

They discuss the evolution of Brooks' product line, highlighting key innovations and the significance of athletes like Scott Jurek in shaping the brand's identity. The conversation also touches on the need for Brooks to embrace more modern marketing strategies and storytelling to better connect with today's runners.

With insights into specific shoe models like the Cascadia and Caldera, they illustrate how Brooks can leverage its legacy while pushing for innovation to regain its status as a leader in the trail running market.

----

Salt Lake Foothills Trail Races

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

---

Instagram

YouTube

Threads

LinkedIn

We've got an app in beta called Wylder by Borderlands. Check it out on iOS or Android.

Brooks Running, a brand that has long been synonymous with running shoes, presents an intriguing paradox in the world of trail running. Despite its extensive availability and established presence, many runners find it difficult to associate Brooks with cutting-edge trail running technology or innovative design.

The conversation between Josh Rosenthal and Taylor Bodeen dives deep into this enigma, exploring the legacy of Brooks, its historical significance in the evolution of trail footwear, and the challenges it faces in contemporary markets.

They discuss the iconic Cascadia model, which has served as a staple for many trail runners, and reflect on how it once dominated the landscape of trail running shoes.

The discussion also touches on the need for Brooks to innovate and revitalize its brand perception, especially as newer brands like Speedland and Norda capture the attention of today's runners with their fresh designs and marketing approaches.

As the dialogue progresses, Taylor highlights Brooks' recent advancements, particularly in foam technology and shoe design, such as the introduction of nitrogen-infused EVA foam and the innovative Catamount series.

These developments signify Brooks' attempts to reclaim its position as a leader in the running community. However, the conversation raises critical questions about the brand's marketing strategies and the visibility of its athletes, suggesting that Brooks could benefit from a stronger narrative that connects its rich history with modern performance expectations.

The episode ultimately paints a picture of a brand at a crossroads, balancing its rich heritage with the need to adapt to an increasingly competitive running shoe market.

Takeaways:

  • Brooks, despite being a household name in running, struggles to maintain its relevance in the trail running segment.
  • The Cascadia remains a staple shoe for many runners, but it lacks the excitement seen in newer brands.
  • Brooks needs to better leverage its athlete endorsements and marketing to connect with consumers.
  • The introduction of nitrogen-infused foam into Brooks shoes represents a significant innovation for the brand.
  • Taylor Bodeen emphasizes the importance of modern design to attract the average consumer.
  • Brooks has the heritage and resources to innovate, but often plays it too safe.
item.content:encoded:
Brooks Running remains a prominent name in the footwear industry, yet it often feels like an enigma within the trail running community.

Josh Rosenthal and Taylor Bodin (Lead Trail Reviewer at BiTR) dive deep into this paradox, exploring why, despite their widespread presence and extensive history, Brooks doesn't quite resonate as a go-to brand for trail runners.

Believe in the Run - Catamount 3

BiTR - Caldera 7

BiTR - Catamount Agil

BiTR - Brooks Divide 5

BiTR - Cascadia 18

They discuss the evolution of Brooks' product line, highlighting key innovations and the significance of athletes like Scott Jurek in shaping the brand's identity. The conversation also touches on the need for Brooks to embrace more modern marketing strategies and storytelling to better connect with today's runners.

With insights into specific shoe models like the Cascadia and Caldera, they illustrate how Brooks can leverage its legacy while pushing for innovation to regain its status as a leader in the trail running market.

----

Salt Lake Foothills Trail Races

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run, 20% off.

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

---

Borderlands

Instagram

YouTube

Threads

LinkedIn

We've got an app in beta called Wylder by Borderlands. Check it out on iOS or Android.

Brooks Running, a brand that has long been synonymous with running shoes, presents an intriguing paradox in the world of trail running. Despite its extensive availability and established presence, many runners find it difficult to associate Brooks with cutting-edge trail running technology or innovative design.

The conversation between Josh Rosenthal and Taylor Bodeen dives deep into this enigma, exploring the legacy of Brooks, its historical significance in the evolution of trail footwear, and the challenges it faces in contemporary markets.

They discuss the iconic Cascadia model, which has served as a staple for many trail runners, and reflect on how it once dominated the landscape of trail running shoes.

The discussion also touches on the need for Brooks to innovate and revitalize its brand perception, especially as newer brands like Speedland and Norda capture the attention of today's runners with their fresh designs and marketing approaches.

As the dialogue progresses, Taylor highlights Brooks' recent advancements, particularly in foam technology and shoe design, such as the introduction of nitrogen-infused EVA foam and the innovative Catamount series.

These developments signify Brooks' attempts to reclaim its position as a leader in the running community. However, the conversation raises critical questions about the brand's marketing strategies and the visibility of its athletes, suggesting that Brooks could benefit from a stronger narrative that connects its rich history with modern performance expectations.

The episode ultimately paints a picture of a brand at a crossroads, balancing its rich heritage with the need to adapt to an increasingly competitive running shoe market.

Takeaways:

  • Brooks, despite being a household name in running, struggles to maintain its relevance in the trail running segment.
  • The Cascadia remains a staple shoe for many runners, but it lacks the excitement seen in newer brands.
  • Brooks needs to better leverage its athlete endorsements and marketing to connect with consumers.
  • The introduction of nitrogen-infused foam into Brooks shoes represents a significant innovation for the brand.
  • Taylor Bodeen emphasizes the importance of modern design to attract the average consumer.
  • Brooks has the heritage and resources to innovate, but often plays it too safe.

item.title: The 215 Mile "Race Across Scotland"
item.itunes:title: The 215 Mile "Race Across Scotland"
item.description:
Join Josh Rosenthal as he dives deep into the transformative power of trail running with coach Kevin Brown, who recently completed the grueling Race Across Scotland.

This conversation highlights the profound connection between running and personal growth, particularly for men seeking to overcome challenges and find purpose in their lives. Kevin shares his journey from a midlife crisis to becoming an inspiring figure in the ultra-running community, emphasizing the importance of building supportive male friendships and holding space for one another.

The discussion also touches on the unpredictability of Scottish weather during the race and the incredible camaraderie that emerges among participants. Through their exchange, listeners will discover valuable insights into how running can serve as a powerful tool for mental clarity, connection, and self-discovery.

Coach Kev's IG

Mind Masters

GB Ultras (Race Across Scotland)

----

May 31, 2025 - Salt Lake Foothills Trail Races

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run, 20% off.

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

----

Josh Rosenthal, the host of the Borderlands Trail and Ultra Running podcast, engages in a heartfelt conversation with Kevin Brown, a coach from Scotland who recently completed the grueling Race Across Scotland, a 220-mile ultra-marathon.

The discussion delves into the transformative power of running, not just as a physical challenge but as a means of emotional and mental release. Brown reflects on his journey from feeling lost in life to finding purpose through ultra running, which he describes as a way to 'park' his daily stresses and process them while on the trail.

The conversation touches on the importance of community and connection among men, emphasizing how relationships can serve as a lifeline for personal growth and well-being.

This episode offers listeners an intimate look at how running can be a vehicle for self-discovery and healing, making it relatable to anyone facing their own struggles, regardless of their running background.

Takeaways:

  • The essence of trail running lies in the connection to nature and the freedom it brings to the mind.
  • Coach Kev emphasizes the importance of male connections for mental health and personal growth.
  • Running allows individuals to process their emotions and build resilience against life's challenges.
  • Community support in running can lead to profound transformations in personal lives and relationships.
  • The race across Scotland serves as a testament to perseverance and the beauty of the journey.
  • Engaging in ultra running is not just about competition, but about understanding one's inner self.

---

Borderlands

item.title: Golden Trail World Series Explained: The Trail Running Competition I Missed
item.itunes:title: Golden Trail World Series Explained: The Trail Running Competition I Missed
item.description:
The podcast dives deep into the Golden Trail World Series, shedding light on its significance in the trail running scene.

Hosts Josh Rosenthal and Brian Peterson discuss how this series offers a thrilling competitive atmosphere, showcasing elite athletes in shorter, intense races that emphasize speed and vertical ascent.

They reflect on a particularly controversial finish at the recent Mammoth Trail Fest, highlighting the fierce desire to win that defines this sport. The conversation also explores the production quality of the Golden Trail events, noting how it elevates the viewing experience compared to traditional trail running broadcasts.

Ultimately, the episode encourages listeners to embrace this growing series and consider its potential to reshape the competitive landscape of trail running.

----

Run the Salt Lake Foothills Trail Races • May 31, 2025 • 10k | Half Merry | 50k

---

Borderlands

Instagram

YouTube

Threads

LinkedIn

We've got an app in beta called Wylder by Borderlands. Check it out on iOS or Android.

----

Takeaways:

  • The Golden Trail World Series has emerged as a competitive platform for trail runners globally, showcasing talent beyond just the American names.
  • Understanding the unique point system of the Golden Trail World Series reveals the strategic nature of this championship.
  • The recent controversial finish at Mammoth highlighted the intense competition and desire to win in trail running.
  • Salomon's sponsorship has significantly impacted the Golden Trail World Series, driving athlete participation and event production quality.
  • The series offers a variety of distances, emphasizing shorter races that still challenge athletes with significant vertical gain.
  • The high production value of the Golden Trail Series events sets a new standard for trail running media coverage.

item.title: The most underrated brand in Trail Running
item.itunes:title: The most underrated brand in Trail Running
item.description:
Taylor Bodin is the Lead Trail Reviewer at Believe in the Run. I'm a big fan of BiTR and beyond stoked to have Taylor join me to deep dive into gear and the history of some storied brands.

A lot of people still see North Face for what it was in the early 2000s and they're missing out on something special. Its effort into trail running shoes is elevating the industry.

Expect to learn about the history of North Face in trail running, the reason shoes have a drop, and a lot more gear geekery.

This one about what happened and this one about the fallout.

We both hope to see Camille restored to her rightful place in ultrarunning but the path back might be filled with obstacles.

item.description:

This episode is perfect for anyone who wants to get to run Leadville. Or for someone who knows they never will but wants to hear all about the iconic course and experience.

Ben Whitehead ran Leadville 100 as his first 100 miler. Is he crazy? Yes. Is that bad? Absolutely not. Ben's crazy was very calculated giving him a solid finish at a very hard race.

We deviate to talk about the rock stars of old like Anton and Hal. Where did all the rock stars go in trail running? Will we have an era like that again?

item.title: Where is the Trail Running Capital of North America?
item.itunes:title: Where is the Trail Running Capital of North America?
item.description:
What are the criteria we should use to crown the capital of North American Trail Running?

Here are some of my thoughts.

Number of elite ultras

Number of non elite ultras

Number of elite runners

Varied trail terrain

Number of dirtbags

Number of bandit races

Trailhead proximity to great restaurants 

Which of these do you like? What would you add?

Why am I doing this?

1. Fun

2. Funner

item.title: What Happened to Leadville 100, Badwater 135, + Dean Karnazes? Remember the Titans.

item.description:
Remember the Titans. Leadville, Badwater, + Dean Karnazes were once the pinnacle of our sport. So what happened?

They're all doing fine. The races are sold out, Dean stays busy.

But when Brian and I started trail running in the early 2010s these titans were celebrated. Not tracking?

Listen as we bring you along making a case that they belong to be restored to their rightful place.

Wolffrunner is back with me on a new episode of Bad Runner's Take.

----

item.title: Is Wasatch 100 the Best 100 Miler in the World? Did UTMB live up to the hype? 3 more things that inspire me.
item.itunes:title: Is Wasatch 100 the Best 100 Miler in the World? Did UTMB live up to the hype? 3 more things that inspire me.
item.description:
Three things.

  1. Wasatch 100 - is this the best 100 miler in the world? I think so. But I'm really biased.
  2. UTMB - Did it live up to the hype? It gave us the best two scenarios possible. A no-name came out of nowhere to win the men's race and the expected exceeded expectations in the women's race.
  3. Inspirition. I live off it and it's hard to find. Here are three things inspiring me right now in trail.

Hayden's Comments

Billy Yang collab

Tommy Lewis

----

item.title: UTMB Week 2024 • Is Trail Running ready for super fandom? UTMB doubled prize money; is that enough? Bad Runners Take.
item.itunes:title: UTMB Week 2024 • Is Trail Running ready for super fandom? UTMB doubled prize money; is that enough? Bad Runners Take.
item.description:
Happy UTMB week.

@wolffrunner joins me on Bad Runners take to talk UTMB.

Forget about the drama surrounding UTMB over the last year for a week (if not forever). If you don't like the juggernaut of UTMB, disregard them.

But don't disregard the 10,000 people running in the mountains around Mont Blanc. This week we celebrate the human spirit taking them from point A to point B, the French culture that surrounds it, and the Heart of Trail Running moving like an avalanche as the running world watches runners take care of business in French/Italian/Swiss Alps.

Brian and I go into topics and recall moments you won't hear anywhere else this year.

  1. Favorite UTMB moments
  2. Is trail running ready for super fandom?
  3. UTMB doubled its prize money. Is that enough?
  4. 2024 picks

----

Brian's interview with Troy on Midpacker Pod.

Billy Yang's UTMB film of Zach Miller + David Laney

----

Sponsors:

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run, 20% off.

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts, Borderlands10 for 10% off.

---

item.title: A flow state not available to trail runners with Jacob Puzey
item.itunes:title: A flow state not available to trail runners with Jacob Puzey
item.description:
I asked the Borderlands audience who I should talk to about my fascination with running a road ultra. The number one suggestion was Jacob Puzey.

After this conversation I understand why.

He brings the perfect insight as a decorated road and trail runner, former coach and trail race director. There's a flow state in running not available to trail runners that comes with repetitive and predictable terrain.

I hope you enjoy this conversation as much as I did.

Jacob Puzey

5 Peaks Trail Running

----

item.title: What do Race Directors do? The Jonas Vingagaard of Trail. The Spirit of Running transcends Borders.
item.itunes:title: What do Race Directors do? The Jonas Vingagaard of Trail. The Spirit of Running transcends Borders.
item.description:
In this episode we explore a Term of Endurance: Race Director. Do we really understand what these RDs put themselves through to put on the local and regional races? They're not full time. It's a massive lift.

I had the good fortune to sit with two local Copenhagen legends, Rasmus and Robin, to talk Danish running and compare Europe running to American running. As much as they're different, the spirit of running transcends politics and borders.

The interview with Robin and Rasmus begins at 14:36.

Rasmus Holst, CEO

Robin Daniels, CBO

Zinsai

----

item.description:
Randi Zuckerberg is new the sport of ultratrail. If you're an old grumpy get-off-my-lawn trail runner you will hate this conversation because Randi is an enthusiastic evangelist for the sport of trail running.

She left the road for trail running about a year ago and dove in head first.

Randi's primary business focus: HUG

item.title: The Myth of Trail Running, the Origin of the 'DNF Before DNF' documentary, and How to Keep Going When the Finish Line is Torn Down.
item.itunes:title: The Myth of Trail Running, the Origin of the 'DNF Before DNF' documentary, and How to Keep Going When the Finish Line is Torn Down.
item.description:
In 2019 Jeremy Cox ran the Buffalo 100 only to finish dead last after the finish line had been torn down. How did he keep going? Why?

We told this story to tell the ultra trail world that every runnerf has a story and no matter how you toe the line, you are a badass and you belong in the trail community.

DFL Before DNF on YouTube.

Ending song: Long Enough by Josh Rosenthal

----

item.title: Is Borderlands Improving Trail Running? What is Believe in the Run doing at Ultratrail Events?
item.itunes:title: Is Borderlands Improving Trail Running? What is Believe in the Run doing at Ultratrail Events?
item.description:
What is Borderlands Trail Running? It's not obvious, I know. Bottom line, I love the trail running industry and want to grow it because I genuinely believe the world will be a better place.

In this episode I explain why Borderlands exists, what its doing, and where its going.

We review the mission, the manifesto, and all the ways I'm trying to send the mission into the ultratrail world.

I bring a new segment called '3 Things' where tell you about three things I love. This will almost always be running but sometimes it may go beyond..

Noted in the episode:

Satisfy x Hoka

Thomas Neuberger + Believe in the Run

----

item.title: Is West Coast or East Coast Trail Running better? The Hellbender 100 with MidPacker Pod's Troy Meadows
item.itunes:title: Is West Coast or East Coast Trail Running better? The Hellbender 100 with MidPacker Pod's Troy Meadows
item.description:
Hellbender 100 in North Carolina is quite a race. Tons of vert and (b)East coast running to the max. Rooty and steep and relentless. It's also now a Hardrock qualifier if that tells you anything about its course profile.

Troy Meadows is the host of the MidPacker Pod and one of my favorites to have on the show. He's insightful and careful with his words. He clearly loves the sport and it shows in this conversation with me and in every conversation he has on his show.

----

item.title: Bad Runners Take | Can the Trail Running Industry Afford to Sponsor Athletes? Did Hayden Hawks Say the Right Thing?
item.itunes:title: Bad Runners Take | Can the Trail Running Industry Afford to Sponsor Athletes? Did Hayden Hawks Say the Right Thing?
item.description:
Elite Trail Running athletes deserve every single dollar a brand will give them in a contract, but is it sustainable? Can the industry support sponsoring athletes?

I don't think the current model is sustainable and this episode goes into depth on why.

Caveat, I'm probably in the top 1% of elite trail running fans. I'm asking this question because I love this sport and want it to grow. But it doesn't help to simply cheer it on as-is. It needs a critical look.

----

item.title: Bad Runner's Take: Is the trail running too young to be a spectator sport? Is a profitable volunteer-ran race the way to go?
item.itunes:title: Bad Runner's Take: Is the trail running too young to be a spectator sport? Is a profitable volunteer-ran race the way to go?
item.description:
This week on a bad runners take:

Big Trail Running • it’s a consolidation of attention around a few names and events but is trail running to new as a spectators sport to do it to this scale?

Volunteer Labor • there’s lots of money to be made in trail running events. Are RDs dependent on volunteer labor? It’s the industry standard but is it ok for a for profit entity to rely on volunteer labor?

Leadville • it exists in its own world and plays by its own rules. And I’m excited for it.

----

item.title: the life of PATH Projects with co-founders Brian and Scott
item.itunes:title: the life of PATH Projects with co-founders Brian and Scott
item.description:
PATH Projects started in 2017 to solve a problem: running shorts. They were mostly designed by shoe companies who are looking to increase average transaction sizes when runners are buying shoes.

In this episode we explore Scott Bailey and Brian Rather's history as founders of  @pathprojects . Scott start SPLIT, Kr3w, and Supra Footwear. Brian started a denim company acquired by Huckberry and went on to develop inhouse brands for Huckberry and others.

The amount of experience here is staggering and since their relaunch in 2023, they're making waves in trail running.

---

Sponsors:

VKTRY Insoles - I wear these every run

PATH Projects - My favorite running shorts

---

More

Borderlands

item.title: Japanese take on American Trail, 100 Miles 100 Times, Caveman Instincts
item.itunes:title: Japanese take on American Trail, 100 Miles 100 Times, Caveman Instincts
item.description:
Tomokazu Ihara is a Japanese Ultratrail runner by way of Tokyo going for 100 100 mile finishes. He first came on my radar after interviewing the RDs of Devils Gulch in Washington. He ran it last year and was one of three finishers in what was a very hot year at DG.

Tomo offers his story and wisdom in this interview and I'm stoked for you to hear it.

----

item.title: Only Three Finishers at Devil's Gulch 100
item.itunes:title: Only Three Finishers at Devil's Gulch 100
item.description:
Devil's Gulch has 3 distances - 1/2 Marathon, 50 Miles, + 100 Miles. In a hot year (2023) DG had only three finishers out of 21 starters.

The course lives up to its name and belongs in the discussion with some of the other big mountain ultras like Wasatch or High Lonesome. 24,000' feet of gain.

It's a beast. It's a devil.

Devil's Gulch

06:45 Exploring the Course of Devil's Gulch

24:26 Hopes for Future Races and Changes

26:21 The Impact of the Race on Runners

28:30 The Challenge and Beauty of Devil's Gulch

31:56 The Support and Community at Devil's Gulch

37:30 Selling Devil's Gulch to Different Runners

----

item.title: Ultramarathon Suffering on Video
item.itunes:title: Ultramarathon Suffering on Video
item.description:
In this conversation, Josh and Ben (of Honey Videography) discuss their experience shooting 'the Zion 100 Miler Broke Me' video for YouTube. They talk about the challenges of capturing the stories and essence of ultra trail running, the gear and equipment required, and the importance of creating compelling content.

Ben shares his impressions of other trail running documentaries and the growing popularity of the genre. The conversation explores the challenges and rewards of creating trail running documentaries.

05:26 The Essence of Ultra Trail Running

10:39 The Growing Popularity of Trail Running Documentaries

20:10 The Evolution of Trail Running Films

22:19 The Importance of Storytelling in Filmmaking

24:09 The Challenges of Filming in the Moment

26:07 The Value of Embracing Failure and Embarrassment

----

Borderlands

YouTube

item.title: The Brutal Last Mile of High Lonesome 100
item.itunes:title: The Brutal Last Mile of High Lonesome 100
item.description:
In this episode, Josh interviews Bri Sullivan about her experience running the High Lonesome 100 race in Colorado. They discuss the beautiful location of the race, the challenging course with high elevation, and the supportive race directors.

Bri shares her personal journey and highlights the sense of community and camaraderie among the runners. They also touch on the the impact of race directors in creating a positive race experience.

----

04:13 The Beauty and Challenge of High Lonesome

10:36 The Impact of Race Directors on the High Lonesome Experience

24:57 Preparing for the High Lonesome 100

27:28 The Impact of Altitude and Breathing Issues

30:19 The Challenging Night Section

35:38 Reaching Purgatory Aid Station and Overcoming Doubts

49:37 Challenges and Highlights of the High Lonesome 100-Mile Race

54:20 The Brutal Finish of the High Lonesome Race

57:31 The Healing and Positive Addiction of Ultra Running

01:05:14 The High Lonesome Race: A Top-Notch Experience

----

item.title: A Heritage American Ultra Race | Palo Duro Trail Run
item.itunes:title: A Heritage American Ultra Race | Palo Duro Trail Run
item.description:
I wasn't an ultra runner until I left West Texas at the age of 19. I am really excited about this episode because I get to think about West Texas through the eyes of one of the older ultras in the country - Palo Duro Trail Run.

In this episode we talk about the history of Palo Duro Trail Run, the region in general, and what makes it beautiful.

This is one of the few ultras from the season I knew I wanted to run before recording a conversation about it. I look forward to toeing the line in Palo Duro Canyon someday to run this race.

Palo Dura Trail Run

Trail Grit, episode 4

----

Borderlands

The ultrarunning human spirit is on full display when the rain is making rivers on the trail and hands are frozen. Enjoy.

item.title: Emma's Race Looked Over at Mile 74, She Finished
item.itunes:title: Emma's Race Looked Over at Mile 74, She Finished
item.description:
Wow, Emma is the embodyment of everything I love about ultratrail running and this story is the epitome of it. Essentially revived from what looked like death or close to it, to a killer second-to-DFL at Wasatch 100 2022.

I was at Ant Knolls and took the photo of her laying on the ground. Fortunatley, you can't DNF at Ant Knoll. She got up, kept going, pushed harder to be the cutoff and got the buckle.

Wasatch100

Borderlands

Instagram.com/runborderlands

item.title: The Surprising Beauty of the No Business 100 Miler
item.itunes:title: The Surprising Beauty of the No Business 100 Miler
item.description:
The No Business 100 is a beast. As I explore races outside of the Western United States I love finding these large communities of ultra runners who love their races. No Business 100 is 14,500' of gain across 102 miles in a section of forest about 60 miles from the Barkley Marathons. Whew.

Heid Baker - Strong Mama Miles

item.content:encoded:
UTMB is beautiful and painful all at the same time. It's got the vert of Hardrock in the setting of the Alps. Everything is different than our American ultras except for the human spirit that propels runners forward. Join Amanda and me as we talk about the full scope of UTMB.

Please take this survey.

item.title: Satisfy Running Co-founder, Brice Partouche
item.itunes:title: Satisfy Running Co-founder, Brice Partouche
item.description:
You might think that you understand Satisfy Running, but would you be open to the possibility that you don't? Listen as Brice Partouche unpacks what he's doing with Satisfy.

Satisfy is among the most talked about brand in trail running right now because it warrants an opinion. You can't dig into it and not emerge with a take on the price or the aesthetic or the marketing or the athletes he sponsors.

If you don't understand French Culture there are some things getting lost in translation.

--

⁠Satisfy⁠

--

item.title: Willie Nelson’s Harmonica Player, Mickey Raphael
item.itunes:title: Willie Nelson’s Harmonica Player, Mickey Raphael
item.description:
Mickey Raphael is among the most quickly recognizable musicians alive today. He's played harmonica with WIllie Nelson since 1973 and has also played with hundred of other greats. At the age of 72, Mickey joins me today to talk about longevity and endurance in career and physical activity.

item.title: MxPx frontman on Endurance + Life | Mike Herrera
item.itunes:title: MxPx frontman on Endurance + Life | Mike Herrera
item.description:
Mike Herrera (of MxPx) has been on more up-mountain grinds with me than anyone else. I have a punk rock mix that helps me feel like a badass when pushing up the opening climb of Wasatch 100 or the big wall in the Virgin Desert. Inside my head I'm a beast externally I'm moving at like a 40 minute mile pace. Still punk rock.

We talked about endurance and pushing through obstacles of all kinds.

——

MxPx is on tour now with the Ataris.

Follow Mike | MxPx

item.description:
Enjoying the podcast? Will you give it a follow and leave a review?

Landy got into Western States 100 and has auto-entry in Wasatch 100 now that he has 6 finishes. He's doing Old Dominion + Vermont as well.

Kaden is doing the Rocky Mountain Slam that culminates at the Bear 100.

These two guys were early adopters of the Borderlands Industry Trail Teams. In this podcast we learn about grand slams and celebrate their ambition.

item.title: 027 • Casey Brady | How to know your body is ok to keep going mid-race
item.itunes:title: 027 • Casey Brady | How to know your body is ok to keep going mid-race
item.description:
Enjoying the podcast? Will you give it a follow and leave a review?

Casey Brady is a physical therapist in Park City, Utah where she trains for 100 and 200 milers, works with athletes, raises children, and runs with Women of the Wasatch. Today we talk about how to know mid-race that you're ok to keep running or not.

item.title: Hal Koerner Inspired a Generation of Trail Runners
item.itunes:title: Hal Koerner Inspired a Generation of Trail Runners
item.description:
Hal and his contemporaries inspired a generation of ultra runners that led to an exponential boom in the sport. Though he was elite, he was also very much a #noPodium runner. Even with back to back Western States wins, Hardrock, etc. he still had a day job balancing a crazy load.

In this episode we talk about the 'good old days'.

-----

Follow Hal + Field Guide to UltraRunning.

Borderlands: Instagram | Youtube | Website

item.description:
Candice Burt founded Destination Races (Tahoe 200, Bigfoot 200, + Moab 240). She talked with us on our season one topic, Late Race Survival.

In 2023 she ran 200 ultramarathons in a row setting a new world record. For her, Late Race Survival is more about Late Day Survival. How did she keep showing up and going forward on day 105 or 144.

She tells us.

item.title: Elite Runner's Advice on Pushing Through Ultramarathons with Anthony Costales
item.itunes:title: Elite Runner's Advice on Pushing Through Ultramarathons with Anthony Costales
item.description:
Welcome to Season 1 Episode 6. This season is all about Late Race Survival. How do the consistent finishers push through the desire to quit? We’re interviewing a bunch of great runners from elite to everyday #nopodium plate spinners.

——

Summary

Anthony Costales, an elite trail runner, joins the podcast to discuss late race survival and his journey from cross country to trail running.

He shares his experiences and insights into pushing through the urge to quit during races. Anthony also talks about his personal life in Salt Lake City and how he balances running with his family and job.

He discusses his race strategy, mindset, and the challenges he faced at Cape Town. Finally, he shares his upcoming race plans for the year.

item.itunes:title: Survivng the Wasatch 100 & Bear 100 Ultramarathons back to back
item.description:
Welcome to Season 1 Episode 5. This season is all about Late Race Survival. How do the consistent finishers push through the desire to quit? We’re interviewing a bunch of great runners from elite to everyday #nopodium plate spinners.

——

Michael Whiteside, an ultrarunner from Ogden, Utah, shares his experiences running the Wasatch 100 and Bear 100 back-to-back. He talks about his background, how he got into ultrarunning, and the challenges he faced during both races.

Despite the physical and mental struggles, Michael pushed through and crossed the finish line. He emphasizes the importance of his support of his crew and pacers in helping him overcome the difficulties.

item.title: 100 Mile Nutrition Tips for Late Race Survival with Jesse Rich
item.itunes:title: 100 Mile Nutrition Tips for Late Race Survival with Jesse Rich
item.description:
Jesse Rich, a nutritionist and coach, joins the DFL Before DNF podcast to discuss late race survival in ultrarunning. Jesse shares his personal running journey, his background in nutrition and coaching, and his decision to not pursue sponsorships. He also talks about his FKT attempt on Millwood and the importance of nutrition in long-distance races. Jesse emphasizes the segmented approach to races and the variability in nutrition strategies for different individuals. Overall, his practical and accessible approach to nutrition and running makes him a valuable resource for runners of all levels.

Takeaways

Segmenting races into smaller goals can help maintain motivation and focus.

Nutrition plays a crucial role in late race survival, and finding a strategy that works for each individual is important.

The decision to pursue sponsorships and become a professional runner is a personal one, and it's okay to prioritize other aspects of life.

DNFs can provide valuable lessons and learning experiences.

Balancing running and coaching can be challenging but rewarding.

Variability in nutrition strategies exists, and what works for one person may not work for another.

Introduction 00:00

Background 02:33

Personal Life and Running Journey 06:15

Education in Nutrition and Coaching 10:26

Balancing Running and Coaching 13:43

Identity as a Runner 17:25

Decision to Not Pursue Sponsorships 22:28

FKT Attempt on Millwood 24:23

DNF Experiences 26:07

Segmented Approach to Races 29:13

Importance of Nutrition in Late Race Survival 32:00

Variability in Nutrition Strategies 37:48

item.title: From DNF to Finish: Insights from Jeremy Cox
item.itunes:title: From DNF to Finish: Insights from Jeremy Cox
item.description:
This podcast delves into the compelling question of how ultra runners like Jeremy Cox find the strength to keep going, even in the face of immense physical and mental challenges.

Host Josh shares his admiration for Jeremy, who has inspired him through his perseverance during grueling races, including the Wasatch 100. The conversation reflects on their shared experiences, including the emotional toll of DNFing (Did Not Finish) and the importance of having a supportive community.

As they explore Jeremy's journey from triathlons to ultra running, listeners gain insight into the mindset required to tackle such daunting distances. The episode emphasizes the significance of resilience and the mental strategies that help runners push through the pain and uncertainty of long races.

Jeremy Cox is the subject of our first documentary called DFL Before DNF. I’m so inspired by him as a runner and person. While we did a deep dive in the doc with Jeremy, there’s still more there. There’s always more there.

Join us in this conversation with data nerd and grit champion, Jeremy Cox where we discuss how to survive the late race carnage.

Follow Jeremy on IG.

---

Borderlands

Instagram

YouTube

Threads

LinkedIn

We've got an app in beta called Wylder by Borderlands. Check it out on iOS or Android.

Takeaways:

  • Jeremy Cox's journey into ultra running began after watching the inspiring story of Rick and Dick Hoyt in the Ironman World Championship.
  • Josh reflects on his own struggles with the 100-mile distance and seeks insights from experienced runners like Jeremy.
  • The podcast highlights the importance of mental resilience in ultra running and how to keep pushing through pain.
  • Jeremy emphasizes the significance of problem-solving skills in endurance sports, comparing it to engineering.
  • Both hosts discuss the balance between training for ultra marathons and family responsibilities, reflecting on their personal experiences.
  • The conversation reveals that the emotional and psychological aspects of ultra running play a crucial role in completing races.

item.itunes:summary:
Jeremy joined us in the La Barba Coffee roasting warehouse to talk about surviving late race carnage.
She got second place at Tahoe 200 and sub-22 hours at Buffalo 100. No doubt she wanted to quit at times so why didn't she? How?!

item.title: How to Survive the Tahoe 200-Mile Trail Race with Elsa Jaworski

Elsa Jaworski joined us in the La Barba Coffee roasting warehouse to talk about late race survival. As co-founder of Women of the Wasatch and a great runner all around, she tells how she keeps going.

Elsa got second place at Tahoe 200 and sub-22 hours at Buffalo 100. No doubt she wanted to quit at times so why didn't she? How?!
